agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from undestroyed WebSocket listeners and missing input validation on untrusted WebSocket data"
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: WebSocket listeners never cleaned up",
      "risk": "Each CourierTracker attaches listeners to the WebSocket but never removes them. There is no `close` handler and no teardown method. When a courier disconnects and reconnects (every ~5 minutes on average), a new CourierTracker is created with new listeners, but the old instance and its closures (which capture `this`, holding references to `courierId`, `ws`, and the `db` module) are never released. The WebSocket object itself may still be referenced by the old tracker's closure. Over 3 months this is the direct cause of the steadily increasing memory usage.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()",
      "reproduction": "1. Connect courier A → creates TrackerA with 2 listeners. 2. Courier A disconnects after 5 min → nothing happens, TrackerA still exists. 3. Courier A reconnects → creates TrackerB with 2 more listeners. 4. Repeat 288 times/day × 90 days = ~25,000 leaked tracker instances per courier.",
      "fix": "Add a `close` listener that removes all event listeners and nulls references. Expose a `destroy()` method. The caller creating `new CourierTracker` must call `destroy()` on the previous instance or the `close` handler must do it automatically:\n\n```typescript\nprivate setupListeners(): void {\n  this.onMessage = (data: string) => { /* ... */ };\n  this.onError = (err: Error) => { /* ... */ };\n  this.onClose = () => { this.destroy(); };\n  this.ws.on('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.on('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws.on('close', this.onClose);\n}\n\npublic destroy(): void {\n  this.ws.removeListener('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.removeListener('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws.removeListener('close', this.onClose);\n}\n```",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability — memory growth will eventually OOM the process"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unguarded JSON.parse on untrusted WebSocket input crashes the process",
      "risk": "Any malformed message from a courier (or an attacker) will throw a SyntaxError from `JSON.parse(data)`. Since this is inside a WebSocket `message` event handler with no try/catch, the uncaught exception will crash the Node.js process (or at minimum kill the connection handler), taking down tracking for ALL couriers — not just the one that sent bad data.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() → ws.on('message', ...)",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g., empty string, binary frame, or `'{invalid'`) over the WebSocket. `JSON.parse` throws, no catch block exists, process crashes.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch and validate the parsed shape:\n\n```typescript\nthis.ws.on('message', (data: string) => {\n  try {\n    const location = JSON.parse(data);\n    if (typeof location?.lat !== 'number' || typeof location?.lng !== 'number') {\n      return; // or log warning\n    }\n    this.updateLocation(location);\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.warn(`Invalid message from courier ${this.courierId}:`, err);\n  }\n});\n```",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — single bad message takes down entire service"
    },
    {
      "title": "No validation on lat/lng values allows database corruption",
      "risk": "Even if JSON parses successfully, there is no bounds checking on `lat` and `lng`. Values like `lat: 999`, `lng: NaN`, `lat: Infinity`, or `lat: null` (if the typeof check above isn't added) will be written directly to the database, corrupting courier location data. Downstream consumers (ETAs, customer maps, dispatch algorithms) will silently produce wrong results.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Send `{\"lat\": 999, \"lng\": -999}` — written to DB with no rejection. All downstream reads for this courier now return impossible coordinates.",
      "fix": "Add bounds validation before the DB write:\n\n```typescript\nprivate isValidCoordinate(lat: number, lng: number): boolean {\n  return Number.isFinite(lat) && Number.isFinite(lng)\n    && lat >= -90 && lat <= 90\n    && lng >= -180 && lng <= 180;\n}\n```",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity — invalid coordinates corrupt dispatch and ETA systems"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "Fire-and-forget DB write with no error handling",
      "description": "db.couriers.updateLocation() is called without awaiting the result or catching errors. If the DB is slow or down, errors are silently swallowed, and there is no backpressure — high-frequency location updates could overwhelm the DB connection pool.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    },
    {
      "title": "No rate limiting on incoming messages",
      "description": "A malicious or buggy courier client could send thousands of location updates per second. Each one triggers a DB write. There is no throttle, debounce, or rate limit — this is a trivial DoS vector against the database.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add a `ws.on('close')` handler as the primary cleanup mechanism — don't rely on callers remembering to call destroy()",
    "Consider throttling updates to at most once per second — GPS precision doesn't justify higher frequency and it reduces DB load significantly",
    "The error handler only logs — consider implementing reconnection logic or at minimum emitting a metric for alerting"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "The memory leak is actively degrading production. Fix should be deployed urgently but is low-risk (additive change — adding cleanup and validation).",
    "rollback_capability": "Fix is fully backward-compatible; rollback is safe at any point.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No breaking changes — all fixes are internal to the handler.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No metrics emitted. Should add: active tracker count, parse error rate, invalid coordinate rate, DB write latency.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the commit — current behavior is strictly worse, so rollback of the fix is safe but undesirable."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak diagnosis — classic Node.js listener leak pattern, directly explains the reported symptom",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic, trivially reproducible",
      "Coordinate validation gap — no code path prevents invalid values"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the caller already manages tracker lifecycle externally (not visible in provided code)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "No external process manager (e.g., pm2) is masking the crash-on-parse by auto-restarting",
      "db.couriers.updateLocation is async and its errors are not caught elsewhere"
    ]
  }
}
